text: The surgeon performs the gastrectomy using a robotic system with two arms, accessed through five trocars strategically placed for optimal maneuverability.
The assistance port is positioned in the left lower quadrant, serving as a crucial access point and extraction site during the robotic near total gastrectomy.
The surgeon initiates the gastrectomy by entering through the gastrocolic omentum, continuing the dissection through this connective tissue to access the stomach.
The surgeon continues the dissection to the patient's right, further opening the gastrocolic omentum, and proceeds upwards along the short gastric vessels to separate the stomach from the spleen.
The surgeon dissects the stomach, freeing it from the ventral surface of the pancreas, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the edge of the colon, moving to the patient's right, and then proceeds cephalad until reaching the duodenum.
The surgeon continues the dissection through the short gastric vessels, maintaining proximity to the spleen to avoid injury.
The surgeon continues the dissection, noting that the stomach is not closely tethered to the spleen, which facilitates the separation process.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to perform dissection, applying the device to control bleeding and separate tissue layers during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the undersurface of the liver, mobilizing the duodenum by carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon opens the gastropathic omentum and continues dissection towards the right crus, proceeding along the ventral surface of the esophagus.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic omentum widely in a stellate pattern, enhancing access for further dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection across the gastroplog vessels, carefully separating these vessels from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and pylorus, ensuring no antrum is retained on the duodenal stump during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon folds the divided duodenal stump over the ventral surface of the pancreas to minimize the risk of a duodenal stump blowout. Subsequently, the more proximal portion of the stomach is being divided.

The surgeon transects the left gastric artery and its nodal arcade, incorporating these structures into the specimen as the dissection progresses toward the right crus and the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon creates a tubular structure of the stomach along the greater curvature, following the dissection and excision steps.

The surgeon staples the stomach and uses bipolar cautery to divide the remaining tissues. The excised specimen is placed into an extraction bag and removed through the port in the patient's left lower quadrant.
The applied port serves as an assistance port and extraction site, with the stomach being maneuvered into proximity with the jejunum.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to create a running suture line along the divided proximal stomach, securing the tissue edges together.

The surgeon completes the back row of the anastomosis, leaving sutures with needles engaged for future use. The jejunum is opened using monopolar scissors, preparing for the ventral portion of the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a barbed monofilament suture to create the inner layer of the anastomosis, placing sutures in the middle of the back row and running them to each corner.

The surgeon begins suturing in the middle of the back row of the anastomosis, sewing to one corner. The suture is then placed again in the middle and sewn to the opposite corner, before being carried around the corner to complete the ventral portion of the inner layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon ties the suture to complete the inner layer of the anastomosis. The suture is then used to finish the second layer, running to the opposite corner and tying off to complete the anastomosis.
An endoscope is inserted into the patient, and saline is liberally applied over the stomach. No air bubbles are observed, indicating no leaks. The excised specimen shows adequate margins.
